Short answer: if you delete `/usr/local/share/jupyter/kernels/python3`, Jupyter should find a default Python kernel using the same Python running Jupyter itself (i.e. Anaconda).

-----

If you just want to fix it, that should be all you need. If you're interested in what's going on, keep reading.

The path you see from `jupyter kernelspec list` is not actually an executable, it's a folder containing a `kernel.json` which tells Jupyter how to start the kernel. That file will look something like this:

```json
{
 "display_name": "Python 3", 
 "language": "python", 
 "argv": [
  "/usr/local/bin/python3", 
  "-m", 
  "ipykernel_launcher", 
  "-f", 
  "{connection_file}"
 ]
}
```

There is a [search path](http://jupyter-client.readthedocs.io/en/stable/kernels.html#kernel-specs) of different directories which a kernelspec can be in. The priority order is user, environment, system. The first one containing `python3/kernel.json` defines the `python3` kernel. As a special case for Python, if no python3 kernelspec is found and it can import `ipykernel`, it offers a python3 kernel running on the same Python executable as the notebook server.

This mechanism may be extended in the future: I have a [plan](https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter_client/pull/261) to make it easier to add other kernel providers, e.g. conda environments.

Here's an attempt to measure the effect, creating a 3x10000 Frame and then saving it either as CSV or as NFF:
```
>>> from time import time
>>> import datatable as dt
>>> d0 = dt.Frame([[1, 2, 3]] * 10000)
>>> d0.shape
(3, 10000)

>>> t0 = time(); d0.save("tmp1"); print(time() - t0)
1.5782227516174316
>>> t0 = time(); d0.to_csv("tmp1.csv"); print(time() - t0)
0.007846832275390625

>>> t0 = time(); d1 = dt.open("tmp1"); print(time() - t0)
0.06298303604125977
>>> t0 = time(); d2 = dt.fread("tmp1.csv"); print(time() - t0)
0.05060100555419922

>>> t0 = time(); d1s = d1.sum(); print(time() - t0)
0.298875093460083
>>> t0 = time(); d2s = d2.sum(); print(time() - t0)
0.07732033729553223
```
As you can see, saving an NFF file takes MUCH longer than saving corresponding CSV (and presumably saving Jay would be as fast as CSV or even faster). Reading NFF is only slightly slower than reading CSV -- but that's mostly because NFF is lazy and does not memmap individual files until they are needed (and with #1130 it'll probably get even faster). Once they're forced to be opened -- NFF is again much slower than CSV.

I tinkered with some custom objects a bit and I see your point about the `keys` attribute:  if it exists and it's not a proper method, `dict(myobject)` breaks.

How about this plan:

Don't implement `keys()`, `values()`, or `items()`.  Then, to protect against the case where a user defines a `key` field, `ModelMeta` detects the issue and automatically does the equivalent of:

```python
class Foo(Model):
    keys_ = ListType(StringType, serialized_name='keys', deserialize_from='keys')
```

I would actually prefer to take it one step further and do something which is not possible to do directly with the current API and alter only the descriptor name and not the key used for the mapping interface.  i.e. create a Model that allows this behavior:

```python
foo = Foo({'keys': 3})
foo.keys_ == foo['keys']
```

We can always guarantee that the mapping interface is correct, so why mess it up?

The biggest issue with this plan is that it silently and automatically fixes the problem, so a user attempting to access their data via `foo.keys` will get an `AttributeError` and be very confused.  Printing a warning every time the model is defined is a non-starter.  So, to inform the user of this niche issue, at the same time that `ModelMeta` renames the descriptor to `keys_`, it will also create a specialized descriptor for `keys` which raises a `AttributeError` that gives the user more information.  e.g. `AttributeError("Model object has no attribute 'keys': the field accessor has been renamed to 'keys_' to avoid conflict with mapping interface")`

It's a little over-cautious, but it would cover all of the edge cases without any serious downsides.

Note that `(fp.isNaN s0)` does not trivially mean that s0 is a constant/value. So, the constant/value propagation will not pick it up. If we replace that with `(= s0 (_ NaN 11 53))` it sees a proper value and it will propagate it, so that this particular benchmark is solved by the simplifier. I've added automatic injection of those auxiliary lemmas to the fpa2bv converter, but this obviously works only for isNaN, not for any of the other fp.is* predicates or other values.

Also, the bit patterns in the significand of case 5 look a little bit special
```
julia> bits(Float64(Rational{BigInt}(1.1854001f0) * Rational{BigInt}(1.9389755f0) + Rational{BigInt}(4.352464f-8)))
"0100000000000010011000110011111111101111111111111111111111101000"
julia> bits(Float32(Rational{BigInt}(1.1854001f0) * Rational{BigInt}(1.9389755f0) + Rational{BigInt}(4.352464f-8)))
"01000000000100110001100111111111"
```
Compare:
```
10011000110011111111101111111111111111111111101000
100110001100111111111
```
(So if there were an extra one at the end "100110001100111111111*1*", rounding `011111...` to `10000...`, then that would give z3's answer with RNE—you can't for example round in two steps, from 53 to 25 to 24.)

```python
def path_after_src(path):
  current_path = path
  path_components = []
  head = None
  tail = ''
  foundSrc = False
  while head != '' and head != '/':
    head, tail = os.path.split(current_path)
    if tail == 'src':
      foundSrc = True
      break
    path_components.append(tail)
    current_path = head

  assert foundSrc
  path_components.reverse()
  return '/'.join(path_components)
```

It won't be tripped up if there are directories inside the `src` directory that contain `src` in their name e.g. `src/silly_src_component_name`.

> ... in a pure c++ world I would have implemented these raw pointers as std::weak_ptr ... We want Python to assume ownership but c++ to avoid it.

Just to add in on Jason's statements, and given these statements in your above post, it seems like simply using `weak_ptr<>` or `shared_ptr<>` in your interface is the most optimal solution; the alternative is to use raw pointers, and hand-craft your lifetime relationships in Python as you would in C++ (something we've considered for some of our stuff in Drake, but nothing we'd wish on our downstream users - to have their interpreter segfault because they forgot to keep a reference alive).

The caveat, as you hinted at before, is that you know you have to shoehorn your object lifetimes into `shared_ptr<>`, which breaks compatibility with non-`shared_ptr<>`-owned objects (which *really* sucks, but alas, such is the drawback of STL); however, that may be a price worth paying to for the future struggling devs. (Another option that someone had mentioned is to consider having two entry points of ownership for your C++ APIs: unique [or shared] ownership for Python-like memory management, and non-owned references for when you want to hand-craft lifetime.)

Wanting C++ to not strictly own the object (in the `unique_ptr<>` sense), but to keep the Python object alive (whether it's a relevant patient object, or actually the Python portion of a Python class subclassing a pybind11 class) is a matter of shared ownership, which as Jason mentioned is exactly what `shared_ptr<>` is meant to do.

AFAICT, there isn't going to be another solution that doesn't involve some sort of shared-ownership mechanism, whether you store a `shared_ptr<>` or a `py::object` reference; another caveat to `py::object` being that you can now have an opaque reference cycle that will defer garbage collection to the death of the interpreter (and may screw up destruction order), since Python doesn't know the connection between your instance and the `py::object` it contains.

(If you do think you have something robust, though, do let us know!)

which is 109 characters. the limit is 108. which is actually a fundamental unix limitation:

```
  Address format
       A UNIX domain socket address is represented in the following
       structure:

           struct sockaddr_un {
               sa_family_t sun_family;               /* AF_UNIX */
               char        sun_path[108];            /* pathname */
           };

       The sun_family field always contains AF_UNIX.  On Linux sun_path is
       108 bytes in size; see also NOTES, below.
```

some additional details -

returns:

```
    {

        source-port: [list]

        protocol: [string]

        other:
            {
            # the most important stuff are listed above, we put those less important here:

            }

        {
        source-address:
        source-prefix:
        source-port:

        protocol:
        other:
            {
            # the most important stuff are listed above, we put those less important here:
```

> I also agree that this can be a bigger undertaking. We can potentially start the process by keeping actions.py in there while directing calls to blockstackd wherever possible and slowly phasing out / merging actions.py.

This is already mostly the case (which leads to the confusing call graphs).  In most methods, `actions.py` connects to `blockstackd` to load and store data.  However, since `actions.py` does a lot of non-trivial input/output processing that is necessary for both clients and servers, `blockstackd` also calls into its copy of `actions.py` as part of `rpc.py`.  The method stubs in `rpc.py` can tell whether it's the server or client calling them, and route the call appropriately.

What needs to happen is the non-trivial logic in `actions.py` needs to go into its own module, so both the client and server can use it without creating weird call graphs.

I'm not sure that this is exactly the same as your original issue.  Here you are getting a conflict originating from the Document `save()`.  It also looks like the conflict stems from the Document `create()` being invoked which should not happen if the document already exists remotely.  While investigating this problem I did uncover another bug ([internal tracking case](https://cloudant.fogbugz.com/f/cases/56912)) in the iteration of documents within a database object.  This may be the cause for your problem but it also may not.  @ewann can you provide a more complete example of the entire block of code you are using that is causing this most recent issue?  

One other thing that may be an issue is my original proposed "work around". 

For example when you do:

```python
my_document = my_database[urllib.quote('Mm5kMTQ0ODQxNTY0MC45OA==', safe='')]
...
my_document = my_database['Mm5kMTQ0ODQxNTY0MC45OA==']
```
You actually are unknowingly adding two members to the locally cached `my_database` object.  One whose key is `'Mm5kMTQ0ODQxNTY0MC45OA=='` and the other whose key is `'Mm5kMTQ0ODQxNTY0MC45OA%3D%3D'`.  Both of which represent the same document.  This could cause problems down the road.  To recify this I am changing my initial workaround to this:

```python
import urllib
doc = Document(db, urllib.quote(unencoded, safe=''))
doc.fetch()
doc.delete()
del doc
```

This is a safer work around that does not cause any unwanted side effects.

One solution is to maintain a version number in `nailgun/__init__.py`. You could access it like this:

```python
>>> from nailgun import VERSION
>>> 'VERSION'
'1.2.3'
```

This could be very useful for two reasons:

* Client code could inspect the version number directly. [1]
* NailGun itself could use this version number for forward/backward compatibility purposes. For example, let's say that the `ServerConfig` object changes and the contents that are dumped out to a configuration file (e.g. `~/.config/nailgun/server_config.json`) change. NailGun might be able to deal with this better if it's able to inspect its own version number and therefore do clever things like have multiple configuration files (e.g. `~/.config/nailgun/server_config_1.2.3.json` `~/.config/nailgun/server_config_2.3.4.json`).

[1] Goodness knows we've had enough issues trying to determine what version Satellite is when testing and troubleshooting - I don't want to make NailGun clients face the same issues. By a similar token, though, it's easy to determine what version NailGun is by asking pip or the system package manager. Sat is a different beast.

My two cents: symbolic execution is applicable for analyzing practical programs, as long as you have enough resource (both computational and storage).

In theory, angr uses more memory than some other symbolic execution solutions for the following reasons:

- We don't have a concrete execution engine. All machine code are executed (simulated) in Python code.
- angr is written in Python, over 99%. It's usually not a memory-efficient solution.
- angr is poorly optimized in terms of resource usage. We hardly have any time for optimizing the memory usage, unless it eats up all memory on our giant server (that has more than 128 GB of memory).
- Z3 solver is in the same memory space with angr itself. For complex constraints, Z3 may take an insane amount of memory, and this is not something angr has control of.

Regarding the reasons above, we have an internal branch in development that supports JIT and concrete execution, as well as some effort to make angr more performant (like some static analyses, separated Z3 solver engine, and the super awesome C++-based angr core). However, we cannot make any promise on if or when those components/functionalities will be available. angr's initial goal was making the problem of binary analysis easier and more approachable. It's not, and will never be a silver bullet to all problems in binary analysis. There are many active researches going on in this field. Expecting angr to be the solution to fundamental problems ("analyzing practical programs", without any limit on program size) is a pure illusion.

Saying that, only having 8GB of memory is too little if you want to analyze any practical code symbolically. I would suggest you get least 32 GB of memory no matter what symbolic execution engine you want to use.

Reading some papers in the field can give you some ideas on how to make symbolic execution smarter. You can start from [Mayhem](https://users.ece.cmu.edu/~arebert/papers/mayhem-oakland-12.pdf).

First, the dxl_io.set_goal_position is actually synchronous. It does wait for the message to be sent to the motor and wait for the answer.

Yet, in the dynamixel they have separated the goal position and the present position. Thus, when you are setting a new goal position using the set_goal_position method it does write the register but then it’s up to the controller inside the motor to do the rest. If you want to know when you reach the goal position, you have to check the value of the present_position. But here again, depending on the torque, the angle limit and the parameter of the pid/compliance margin/slope you may never reach the set goal position.

Precisely knowing when you should reach the goal_position is tricky as the acceleration curve depend on many parameters. You can find their theoretical values on the dynamixel website, but the real ones can really differ :) There is no internal mechanism in the dynamixel that would, for instance, raise a callback when the goal position is reached.

Finally, I can’t really say there is a better or worse way to control motors. It really depends on what you are aiming to do :) I’m not sure to understand what you would like to do.

BTW, do not hesitate to directly post on the poppy forum or the GitHub issue tracker other people may be also interested in your question!

If you could provide a reproduction (including stdout, and the patch file if applicable) that would be very helpful!

In a worst-case, `pre-commit` saves a diff of the unstaged contents (and prints the location of the patch file) which can be used to restore the state if pre-commit doesn't do it itself.

The behaviour is actually desirable though, when committing you want the hooks to test the contents of files _as they're being committed_ and not take into account unstaged changes (this is a rather glaring problem with the usual naive approach).  As such, pre-commit saves off a patch of the unstaged diff, clears the staged area, runs the hooks, and then (if everything is working properly!) restores the patch.  Rest assured, the intention is not to leave your repository in a different state than found :)

One very ugly workaround that we did find (though likely won't use) is to simply fool the importer into skipping over `pkg_resources`. Somewhat surprisingly, this works at the top of a CLI script:

```
# This needs to be the first thing you do. Obviously, if `pkg_resources` is already imported you are too late!
import sys
sys.modules['pkg_resources'] = None
<do work>
del sys.modules['pkg_resources']
```

I'm not necessarily advocating this in all cases but I'll leave it here as a possible workaround for others.

That said, I would still really like to hear from the `setuptools` folks on this if possible. Shoot.  Yeah, I have an idea what's going on.  Thanks for reporting this.  I'm surprised this issue has slipped through the cracks.

This is a "feature" of Python 3.4.  When inspecting a callable, it tries to report the number of required arguments even for builtin C extensions.  In other versions of Python, this inspection returns -1 if it can't determine the number.  Python 3.4 lies if it can't determine the number: it returns 0.  It even returns 0 for `map`, `filter`, etc.

I have explored an explicit way around this in #46 and pytoolz/toolz#202.  This made execution of `curry` *slightly* slower, but I think this may be worth it.  Two other options are to update the heuristics to `curry` to not raise the TypeError if the numargsrequired is 0, or to explicitly list all the curried cytoolz exceptions in the `known_numargs` workaround in `functoolz` for this issue.  I favor the explicit and generally applicable `numargs` keyword to `curry`.

1.  turn on **Use VTE titles for tab names**

1. 1. optional turn off **Abbreviate directories in tab names**

1. Tab name changed to "Terminal"

I think tests 0 to 2 work as intended and documented.

From what I can tell about test 3, **Use VTE titles for tab names** respects user set names but **Abbreviate directories in tab names** does not (only at the moment of turning it on). **Abbreviate directories in tab names** should not touch names for terminals which have been set by the user. In `gconf-editor` you can add a key under `apps/guake/general` with the name `startup_script` and a path to your script.

Example:

```
#!/bin/bash

/usr/bin/guake &
sleep 2 # let main guake process start and initialize D-Bus session
guake -r "~/projects/foobar" -e "cd ~/projects/foobar"
guake -n " " -r "ssh:some-host" -e "ssh some-host"
guake -n " " -r "ansible" -e "cd ~/projects/ansible"
guake -s 0
```

The options are documented under `guake --help`

There are two problems here:

1. Our communication is language dependent, that is to say, the "same" information passed from `sos` to `matlab` can be presented quite differently from `sos` to `R`. We will have to either use a "general" format (such as JSON) to make our presentation language-neutral, or expand the interface to something like `sos_export(var, to='R')`.

2. The recipient language needs to have this `from_sos` function defined somewhere, so the script will most likely something like 
```
library('sos')
var=from_sos(filename)
```
That means we will have to write modules for each language we support.

If we do not want to support two sets of mechanisms, we can implement these modules and let the language modules use this mechanism as well. Other than performance losses, we will at least lose the feature of direct variable transfer between subkernels (not by way of sos).

There's two problems here. Only the first one is shown in the error message but the second problem will manifest as soon as we fix the first.

1. We assume the existence of `RAG.node[node_idx]['labels']`, containing a list of labels pertaining to that node. We do this basically throughout the code, [even in `RAG.merge_nodes`](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/future/graph/rag.py#L213), without requiring that it be added at construction time. This is a major issue and should probably change. My preference is to somehow remove the assumption, because in many applications, it's not really warranted to maintain these lists: the node is the label. However this may not be possible, in which case we should add the lists to the constructor.

2. Even if we fix that, the next part of the ncut code will look for a `weight` attribute on each edge. When a `weight` attribute is not found, the RAG should produce a meaningful error message. Not really sure how to make this one happen.

The bug/improvement in `coverage combine` would be that when it doesnt find multiple coverage files, but does find a normal coverage file, it doesnt create an empty coverage file.

What is the benefit of `coverage combine` replacing one coverage file with an empty coverage file...?

However, I see at https://bitbucket.org/ned/coveragepy/issues/511/version-42-coverage-combine-empties that this is at least partially intended behaviour introduced for coverage 4.2+

And the solution there is to use `coverage combine --append` on coverage 4.2+ if we dont want to overwrite the existing coverage data file.

I have raised https://bitbucket.org/ned/coveragepy/issues/516/running-coverage-combine-twice-deletes-all about the side-effect we are experiencing.

> What the client should do is run coverage xml and if no data is found run coverage combine && coverage xml as a fallback. 

Aye, that could work.

Another approach is for `codecov` to check for `.coverage` /  `.coverage.*` files, and then only run `coverage combine` if there are partial coverage files and no unified `.coverage`.
It can then also emit messages if it finds both, which suggests that some partial coverage data needs to be appended, but IMO codecov shouldnt automatically do that merge.  (I would be happy to write the PR for this approach.)

You'll need to elevate privileges to Administrator for that python process.  There's no way around that if you install conda in multi-user mode.  Multi-user mode means the base environment is Administrator-owned, and therefore modifying the base environment can only be accomplished with administrator privileges.  However, you can create, activate, and modify new, non-base environment when the base environment is read-only.  *That* is what multi-user mode is all about.

> I need to be able to trigger a conda update from within Python running without elevated privileges.

Then you'll have to install conda/miniconda for your current user, so that your current user has write permissions for the environment.

> It seems that a multiuser install on macOS does not have this kind of issue.

Not true.  Same situation.  On macOS you'd also need to elevate privileges to update the base environment when the base environment is read-only to the current user.

Consider a per-test-method tear down that logs externally:

```python

    def expectThat(self, matchee, matcher, message='', verbose=False):
        """ An assertion helper method.

            Args:
                matchee: An object to match with matcher.
                matcher: An object meeting the testtools.Matcher protocol.
                message (str)
                verbose (bool)

            Returns:
                None
        """
        self.logger.debug('called expectThat, ' + message)
        # broad wrapper around TestTools `expectThat` method for additional
        # conditions and logging.
        super(CouncilTestCase, self).expectThat(matchee, matcher, message, verbose)

    def tearDown(self):
        """ TearDown a test method defined in a TestCase class. """

        self.logger.debug('test-case teardown')

        interfaces = self._result_interfaces()

        if interfaces:
            self.logger.debug('logging pass/fail details to (%d) interfaces' % len(interfaces))

            # if we have a results instance class defined
            # then we want to log these test results to that database
            # using its interface.

            details = self.formatted_details(misc=self._misc_to_dict())

            if self.failed:
                row_id = self.__log_failed(details, interfaces)
                self.push_status('Failed', {'done': True, 'passed': False}, 100)
            else:
                row_id = self.__log_success(details, interfaces)
                self.push_status('Passed', {'done': True, 'passed': True}, 1)

            self.push_status('Logged to database', {'database_rowid': row_id})

        self._auto_teardown(None)
        self._with_driver = False
        self._clear_misc(delete_persist=False)

        super(CouncilTestCase, self).tearDown()
```

You know whether or not the whole method passed using standard `testtools.TestCase` idioms -- but, we don't know which `expectThat` methods passed/failed. We can infer which ones failed by pulling them from `.getDetails()`.

It would be much easier to know, per `expectThat` call if the deferred assertion was a success or failure while the test is running; for long running tests that may not even get to the end of the method.

By simply returning `True` or `False` it would avoid the infer step, as well as parsing the messages out of `.getDetails()`

```python
        for key, value in detail_items:
            # iterate through all of the details
            # trying to assign them in buckets with like-typed objects

            if key.lower().startswith(config.mismatch_error_str):
                # filter out all of the MismatchErrors, logged by a call
                # to :fun:`.expectThat`.
                split_msg = value.as_text().split(':', 2)

                if len(split_msg) == 2:
                    # no message parameter passed, fill with a blank
                    split_msg.append('')

                # take of all the white spacing
                split_msg = list(map(lambda x: unicode(x).strip(), split_msg))

                _, err_str, msg_str = split_msg

                mismatches.append('%s,%s' % (err_str, msg_str))
                continue
```

At this level of generality, the command will need the notification token as input. That means, before this makes sense, we need CLI commands that return these tokens. We don't deal with asynchronous job completion in the CLI,so the only remaining possible notifications are:
- object (property/status) changes
- audit
- security
- os message channel

It seems to me that these areas should be handled separately in the CLI. That allows hiding the notification topics.

* `zhmc partition show-os-messages <cpc-name> <partition-name> [-f]`
* `zhmc lpar show-os-messages <cpc-name> <lpar-name> [-f]`

@mmerickel I had a quick look into this and there is one thing that concerns me.

gearbox serve has `--monitor-restart` option which will restart the server if it crashes and doesn't seem it would easily integrate with hupper without having 2 huppers running.

The `--monitor-restart` feature pretty much spawns a subprocess and checks if it's still running, if it dies it respawns it.

Once hupper gets integrated hupper can be in two different situations:

1. It can be the parent of the `--monitor-restart` process
2. It can be the child of the `--monitor-restart` process

Seems both cases would have some problems that are not easy to work around:

1. As hupper is the parent of the `--monitor-restart` process it would detect code changes in gearbox but not in the web application as it monitors `sys.modules` and the application modules are not loaded in the `--monitor-restart` process as it only acts as an angel the process actually serving the app is an inner one.
2. As hupper is the child of `--monitor-restart` process it would properly detect code changes, but it looks like in this case the `--monitor-restart` process would be unable to detect a server crash as it would be monitoring hupper and looks like hupper doesn't quit if the server crashes. See:

```
import time
import hupper


def main():
    if not hupper.is_active():
        hupper.start_reloader('restarttest.main')
        return

    for i in range(1, 4):
       print 'Counting %s...' % i
       time.sleep(1.0)

    print 'Crashing... now hupper will hang forever'
    raise RuntimeError('e')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

I would like to go for solution *2* as it seems a more natural structure, but it's odd that hupper hangs forever instead of quitting if the process it's monitoring crashes.

> They are theano operations, but with the sanity of keeping shape information and separating function from implementation.

True. Possibly the only reason holding us back from allowing things such as `T.dot(layer1, layer2)` is that Theano doesn't have shape inference, and it seemed easier to write Lasagne than to fix this.

> I often need to go back in the code because I treated lasagna layers as Theano tensors.

> https://github.com/317070/kaggle-heart/blob/master/deep_learning_layers.py#L268

That's a bit more complicated, you're broadcasting by padding the shape on the right rather than the left (as standard broadcasting rules would do). > Isn't there a way to do this with broadcasting or something?

Yes, if I see correctly, what Søren does is partly equivalent to `return input.dimshuffle(0, 'x', *range(2, input.ndim))`.
The problem is that Søren sometimes explicitly needs a tensor of the given shape to be fed to `scan`, and Theano doesn't offer a functionality similar to `np.lib.stride_tricks.as_strided` -- that would allow you to define a tensor with a given shape, but some strides set to zero so it implicitly repeats over those dimensions (like in broadcasting, but for a specific numer of times).

> It seems unnecessary to copy the same thing n times.

Depending on what you're doing with the tensor, you may actually require it to be explicitly repeated (so you can modify elements in place without affecting their repetitions). Otherwise we could ask upstream how difficult it would be to add something like `as_strided` to Theano. I guess it would require a lot of changes...

Would `UndoLayer` be better? A more accurate name would be `GradientLayer` or something, but then it's not so clear what it's useful for. Other possibilities are `ReverseLayer` or `BackwardLayer`, but they could be confused with doing something to time sequences.

What about the location? Shall we start a `lasagne/layers/autoenc.py` for that?


@dsully 

> Doesn't it make sense to drive this off of asyncio / trollius / aiohttp at this point? Or perhaps even curio?

1. Doing IO asynchronously inherently brings about a more complicated programming model. In fact, if you watch some of the PyCon presentations about some of the newer async io frameworks for Python, you'll notice how people continuously highlight that a main feature of them is that **they make async programming look more like threads**. So I would reverse the questions and ask, "Why should we use something that tries to make a complicated programming model look like threads, when we can just use .... threads?"
2. Async io shines under the very specific circumstance where you want to do **massively** parallel io bound operations, like the famous C10k problem (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C10k_problem). I can't think of a single task to be performed with SoCo that would fit that description and therefore would benefit from async io. However if someone can present one, I would happily listen and we could reconsider.

Using twisted does solve the problems raised in this thread, but I am not at all convinced that they cannot be solved using threads. However, since the issue has existed for some time now, it is of course awesome that @relevitt has shared this workaround.

I looked into this more as I am beginning more serious work with the SDK, and ran into a few issues with the iWorkflow REST proxy as it is when used with BIG-IQ CM. 

In `_get_device_uuid` the code assumes that the devices are located in the device group `cm_cloud_managed_devices` which I don't believe exists in BIG-IQ by default. The SDK is hard-coded so adding other groups is a problem.
https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/af2746d73d7f888cf64fe7db8dfe254867ceafd7/f5/iworkflow/__init__.py#L178

A workaround in BIG-IQ is to create the group `cm-cloud-managed-devices` by sending a POST with the needed json payload. (Creating it via the webui results in the name of the group using underscores instead of dashes. )
```
{ "autoManageLocalhost": true,
  "displayName": "cm-cloud-managed-devices",
  "groupName": "cm-cloud-managed-devices",
  "infrastructure": false,
  "isViewGroup": false,
  "kind": "shared:resolver:device-groups:devicegroupstate",
  "properties": { "cm:gui:module": [ "adc",
                                       "device",
                                       "networksecurity",
                                       "asmsecurity"]},
  "selfLink": "https://localhost/mgmt/shared/resolver/device-groups/cm-cloud-managed-devices"}
```

I found an additional issue when attempting to create the group as a dynamic type and telling it to include all devices in BIG-IQ. If the group is a dynamic type, it seems to be returned as a different type of object, and `_get_device_uuid` fails with `AttributeError: '<class 'f5.iworkflow.shared.resolver.device_groups.cm_cloud_managed_devices.Device'>' object has no attribute 'pop'
`
I added a try/except block which catches the issue:
https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/af2746d73d7f888cf64fe7db8dfe254867ceafd7/f5/iworkflow/__init__.py#L196
```
            try:
                return resource.pop('uuid', None)
            except AttributeError:  # needed if group is a dynamic group
                return resource.uuid
```

> I was not aware yet that scipy was deprecating their imageio functionality and putting imageio forward as a replacement.

_Yup._ This is a pivotal moment for imageio. I believe `scikit-image` is also [in process](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/1605) of transitioning their backend from PIL to imageio. <sup>_no pressure though_</sup>

Congratulations on the increased attention this project has deservedly earned!

> we may want to add a common option (i.e. keyword argument) to all formats that the Pillow plugin provides.

👍 

> Perhaps not for mode, since that seems specific to Pillow,

👍 👍 👍 

Technically, it might be feasible to try to cobble together alternative implementations of the `mode` keyword for non-Pillow plugins. Pragmatically, doing seems undesirable. It's doubtful that Pillow's implementation of `mode` would exactly conform to that of imageio's for non-Pillow plugins. Data returned by `imread()` would then conditionally depend on which plugin the end user is currently using.

Which... kinda seems bad.

Which leads me to another question, actually. Is it feasible for users to specify not merely *soft* preferences but *hard* requirements for formats? Specifically, is it feasible for users to *require* (rather than merely *prefer*) that only Pillow plugins be used?

Since non-Pillow plugins would be unlikely to support the Pillow-specific `mode` parameter, imageio would probably raise a low-level exception (as you helpfully suggest earlier). That's reassuring. Still, explicit support for specifying mandatory dependencies *would* permit imageio to raise a more human-readable exception. <sup>in theory</sup>

At the moment, the following snippet appears to be the best approximation:

```
try:
    import PIL
except:
    raise ValueError('Mandatory dependency "Pillow" unimportable.')

import imageio
imageio.formats.sort('-PIL')
```

Perhaps a new `imageio.formats.require()` function passed the exact same positional and keyword arguments as the existing `imageio.formats.sort()` function could be added to the API. `require()` might iteratively validate all passed formats to be usable under the current environment, raising an exception if not *or* deferring to `sort()` if so. Maybe?

But... what do I know. I'm just a disembodied string of characters on a glowing phosphorescent screen.

> there might be some complexity in that regard.

May the overly complex source be with you. I feel your pain.

> I can see `mode` working for pillow-formats

**Right.** `mode` is absolutely appropriate. It's also a little... ambiguous. Maybe? I can't imagine Pillow is the only image framework to leverage that term. That said, I have a poor imagination. Maybe `pil_mode`, `mode_pil`, or something similarly Pillow-specific?

How *does* imageio handle keyword argument conflicts, actually? I assume it would be A Pretty Bad Thing™ if competing plugins for the same format (e.g., `PNG-FI` and `PNG-PIL`) both accepted a `mode` argument with different (and hence) conflicting interpretations. `</collective_shrug>`

> I would argue in favor of renaming the grayscale option to `as_gray`

I would argue identically. `as_gray` is particularly nice, in that the `skimage.data.imread()` function accepts a parameter of the same name. Conformance assists sanity, which is nice.

> We could consider providing an alias `flatten` that prints a warning (or raises an error) telling the user to use `as_gray` instead.

`flatten`. *Yeah.* From the usability perspective, I definitely see where you're coming from here. From the maintainability perspective, I shudder at bit rot, code smells, and *yet more* maintenance burden.

Developer time is precious. Perhaps imageio documentation could instead synopsize this change in human-readable text (e.g., "Substitute `flatten` for `as_gray`.")? Especially if *this* happens:

> We might also setup a small doc page to help people in their transition.

👍 

Thanks for the attentive ear, @almarklein. There's no immediate rush on any of this, of course. Best to get sweeping API generalizations right the first time, eh? <sup>_My Canadian dialect betrays me even online._</sup>

@a1k0n ,@erikbern
Thanks guys for your comments. I just brought up this paper because it is using a sample of two points to estimate a global property of the dataset (in their case the diameter of a dataset). You are also using two points towards a similar goal but differently.  I saw as Andy pointed out that Dasgupta use x and y to figure out the split point only but not the direction, while you use x and y for the direction. I would think that your rule makes more sense.
I am not sure if Dasgupa's rule will bring something, but just to clarify his argument.
His setup is that there are two clusters (this is his figure 2), Bi and Bj which are with small diameter but somewhat further apart [a bit stronger assumption]. He wants to separate them cleanly. He is saying that by adding a random offset to the median we can improve the chance of a good split. Bi and Bj themselves live in a top level clusters and he uses x and y to find the diameter of the top level cluster.

Under your rule if you have two clusters, you will have a good split if you pick x and y to be from different clusters. I think this rule works better when you have a larger diameter (more dimensions in the dataset, more data points, more diverse data points). So it's interesting to see if the effectiveness of the rule diminishes in lower nodes of the tree.
I know that when you do euclidean distance you try a couple of directions and choose the best (the one with the widest projection). In conjunction with the new rule, this will mean that you also want x and y to be further apart.  Together both rules will imply that you will select the split points to be from different "clusters". 
One thing that I saw that people do in a similar context (PCA-trees) is when they choose a direction d (in your case x - y) they subtract it from the data. This gives them the property that
subsequent splits are orthogonal. I'm keen to try this myself. I'm planning to subtract the previous direction from the new direction (not from the data) which is obtained by sampling two new points. I think here is also safe to require that ||x - y|| is not too small because the orthogonality will give rise to a different split than at the parent level. When ||x - y|| is small the rule approaches the random hyperplane rule, so it might not be too bad (as long as x - y does not have too many zeros).

I'm pretty excited about your methods because it has quite nice properties: 1) cheap; 2)it has randomness; 3) it is informed by the data.

Cheers,

Stefan

So to @josegonzalez's point, maintaining all of our plugins was a real burden. There's just so many different things and so much different software that Diamond can monitor, that it's unbearable to maintain in core Diamond.

A while back, I pushed to slim down core Diamond to essentially just basic system monitoring, and provide individual repositories for each plugin, so they can be maintained independently by people who have context, etc. But these efforts were stalled and there wasn't a ton of interest.

This would help make Diamond as a project more sustainable: https://github.com/python-diamond/Diamond/issues/183

With that said, I put in effort into getting the structure in place, as commented: https://github.com/python-diamond/Diamond/issues/183#issuecomment-223072250 with an example of splitting out the Redis collector.

The thing with CURIEs is that you always need a manager which knows the namespace mapping... it's tempting (and i've done it a couple of times to just use one pre-filled from http://prefix.cc), but you get into a lot of trouble if you start serializing without the prefix definitions and then want to parse later on. Namespaces change over time (dbpedia vs dbp vs dbr vs dbpr)!  It might be that you have a legit use-case for this, but it's as likely that you're getting yourself into trouble in the long run by not using a proper serializer and parser.

My only concern here is about simplicity:

1. The way developers interact with XML wrappers should not differer from one to another. This means that developers should always know what to expect after a XML response is returned.
2. Final users of wrapper should not have to worry about configuring extra things before making a call. If ``xmltodict_unparse__``  and ``xmltodict_parse__`` will only be used by the developer of the wrapper for configuring specific service behaviour, that's ok. It's only a matter of properly documenting/explaining this parameters and pointing them to ``xmltodict`` documentation.

`mx.model.FeedForward.create` is a function intended for training of the model, it is therefore expected to have a label passed as the `y` argument. 

Your model should have a loss function for in order to train; for example, `mx.symbol.LinearRegressionOutput`. Please take a look at this MNIST tutorial for example:  http://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/tutorials/r/mnistCompetition.html. 

You need to be aware of the the data dimensions through the model: if 126 samples in X, Y should also be of length 126. Check the model arhcitecture used: a FC on images (which should normally be of equal WH dimensions) is likely not what is needed (check for convolution layers). You are CPU limited - Alexnet is a very small network and Titans are fast. How many cores do you have on your CPU? If more than 4 then try to set `--data-nthreads N` where N is number of threads to do IO. By default MXNet uses only 4 cores to do IO. You may also limit augmentations you perform (by default train_imagenet script performs a lot of augmentations).
I am in the process of improving image IO (#7152), that should also help.
